title: Everything Is Down
blurb: Whole clinic dark at open. Patients in the lobby. Go.
hardBlurb: no computers no schedule PATIENTS ARE HERE. call the front desk the moment you see this.

n1.text:
07:58. Your phone rings before you've even sat down. It's Renee, the office manager at Lakeside Family Medicine:

"NOTHING is working. No computers, no schedule, nothing — and patients are already checking in. I need this fixed NOW."
n1.hint: Blast radius first: one user, one site, or one service? Everything about your next ten minutes depends on that answer.

choices.label: "Okay — first question: is it just your computer, or is everyone affected?"
choices.feedback: Scope first, always. One user, one site, or one service — everything about your next ten minutes depends on that answer, and it costs five seconds to ask.

choices.label: "Let me remote into your PC right now and take a look."
choices.feedback: Action feels good and Renee hears urgency — but you're about to diagnose one machine before you know the blast radius. If the whole site is down, her PC tells you nothing.

choices.label: "Whoa, slow down. Open a ticket and we'll get to it in order."
choices.feedback: A clinic-wide outage with a lobby full of patients IS the front of the queue. Matching your urgency to theirs is a people skill — and this answer tells the client you don't get it.

n2.text:
Renee checks around: front desk, both exam wings, the billing office — every screen is locked out of everything.

"It's everyone. The whole building."
n2.hint: You already have a tool that can see every Lakeside machine at once — no phone tree required.

choices.label: Pull up the RMM dashboard and check Lakeside's agents.
choices.feedback: Monitoring answers in seconds what phone calls answer in minutes. Every agent offline at one site = site-wide, and almost certainly network or power — not thirty broken PCs.

choices.label: Ask Renee to check the cables behind her PC.
choices.feedback: Her desk cables can't take out three departments. You're pattern-matching to the wrong scale — and burning the goodwill of your only pair of hands on site.

choices.label: Escalate to L2 immediately — a whole site down is above L1.
choices.feedback: Escalating a site-wide P1 is never wrong — but escalating with zero triage means L2 starts from scratch. Spend three minutes gathering facts first and you hand them a running start.

n3.text:
RMM: every Lakeside agent has been offline since 07:41. You try the clinic's firewall from outside — no response to ping.

So: internet out? Firewall dead? Something else?
n3.hint: Dead from the outside could be ISP, power, or the edge device. Someone standing in the building can rule two of those out in seconds.

choices.label: "Renee — are the desk phones working? And is anything beeping in the closet where the network gear lives?"
choices.feedback: Dead agents + dead firewall + (maybe) dead VoIP points at power or the edge device, not the ISP. The customer is your remote hands — good techs use them early and kindly.

choices.label: Call the ISP and open an outage ticket.
choices.feedback: Reasonable guess — but from the outside, a firewall killed by a power blip looks identical to an ISP outage. Rule out the closet first; the ISP hold queue will eat 20 minutes either way.

choices.label: Start restoring the firewall config from last backup.
choices.feedback: You've diagnosed nothing and you're already doing surgery. Never reach for the most invasive fix before you know what's actually broken — you can turn a 30-minute outage into a 3-hour one.

n4.text:
Renee walks to the closet. "There's a black box making a horrible beeping noise, and none of the little lights I usually see are on."

The UPS failed overnight — and when the power blipped at 07:41, it took the firewall and switch down with it.
n4.hint: The best bridge fix is safe, reversible, and within reach of the hands you have on site. And people calm down when they hear an ETA in plain words.

choices.label: Guide her through moving the firewall and switch onto wall power, give her a plain-English ETA, and log it in the ticket.
choices.feedback: Bypassing a dead UPS is a safe, reversible bridge fix that's within L1 reach. Pairing it with an ETA in normal human words and a ticket note — that's the whole job in one move.

choices.label: Tell her to hold the UPS power button until the beeping stops.
choices.feedback: That silences the alarm, not the outage — the gear still has no power. And a failing UPS mid-fault can behave unpredictably. Fix the outage first; deal with the noise after.

choices.label: Wait for L2 — you shouldn't be directing someone around power equipment.
choices.feedback: Caution has its place, but "unplug from the dead box, plug into the wall" is safe and standard. A clinic lobby filling with patients is not the place for wait-and-see.

n5.text:
08:22. Gear is on wall power. Agents are coming back online one by one, and you can hear the front desk unfreezing in the background.

Renee: "Okay, the schedule is loading… are we good?"
n5.hint: Back online isn't the same as done. What stops this exact call from happening again next month?

choices.label: Confirm each department is back, tell Renee you'll check in again in 30 minutes, and flag the dead UPS for replacement.
choices.feedback: An outage isn't over when things turn on — it's over when the client hears you say so and the root cause has a follow-up. That UPS WILL fail again; today it's a line item, next month it's another P1.

choices.label: "You're good! Closing the ticket."
choices.feedback: Close it now and the next power blip is a repeat P1 with a angrier opening call. No recovery confirmation, no root-cause note, no follow-up — three misses in four words.

choices.label: Escalate the UPS replacement to L2 and move on to your next ticket.
choices.feedback: The follow-up instinct is right — but leaving without closing the loop with Renee leaves the ticket emotionally open even though it's technically resolved. Thirty seconds of "we're good, here's what happened" buys months of trust.

n6.text:
You write it up: timeline, root cause (failed UPS), fix (bypassed to wall power), follow-up (replacement quote to the account manager).

Renee's last message: "Thank you — you kept us calm."

Ticket closed.
